blocks.content: Which means you didn't have time to decide not to.
The words sat in the quiet of his room long after the screen went dark.
Ethan looked up at the ceiling.
And for a moment, the ceiling was different.
Older.
Textured plaster. A water stain he used to pretend was a map.
He was nine.
Ten, maybe.
The house wasn't loud that night. That was the thing about it. When his parents fought—really fought—there was no warning. The house just went from warm to hollow between one breath and the next.
He remembered sitting at the top of the stairs.
Just the sound of voices below.
Not the words.
He'd learned not to listen to words. Words could be taken back. Words could be explained away.
But tone—
Tone didn't lie.
And the tone that night had the sound of something ending.

He'd gone back to his room. Closed the door softly, because something in him already understood that making himself small was the safest option. Sat on the edge of his bed the same way he was sitting now.
And decided.
Don't give anything the chance to matter that much.
It wasn't a child's thought. He hadn't had it that cleanly.
But his body understood it.
And slowly, over the years that followed, it became the architecture of him.
The way he moved through rooms without leaving marks.
The way he kept friendships comfortable but not close.
The way he painted the world at a careful distance—observed, rendered, never absorbed.
Nathan had seen through it from the start. That was the infuriating thing about Nathan.
"You love everything you won't let yourself love," he'd said once, junior year, after Ethan declined an invitation for the fourth time in a row.
Ethan had told him to mind his business.
Nathan had laughed.
"That IS my business. I'm your business."
Ethan almost smiled at the memory.
Almost.
His phone buzzed.
You still there?
Yeah.
You went somewhere.
Just thinking.
About her?
About her.
About every reason not to.
About the way those reasons felt thinner tonight than they ever had before.
About a lot of things.
She worth it?
Ethan looked across the room at Gerald.
The duck stared back, unqualified, unbothered, completely unhelpful.
I think so.
Then stop thinking.
She sounds like the kind of person you meet once.
Ethan felt the weight of that settle somewhere quiet.
Because he knew.
He already knew.
Yeah.
Then show up tomorrow.
Don't think about it. Just show up.
That's what you're supposed to do.
Ethan set the phone down.
Lay back.
Stared at the ceiling.
The fountain sketch was still on his desk. He could see it from here. The figure near the easel—barely suggested, still not erased.
He thought about what she'd said.
Trying is underrated.
He thought about the way she smiled when he laughed.
Like she'd been waiting for it.
Like it mattered.
For the first time in a long time, Ethan didn't reach for a reason to stay quiet.
He just closed his eyes.
And let tomorrow feel like something worth walking toward.
